subject: Physics
q: Two fixed frictionless inclined plane making an angle $30^{\circ}$ and $60^{\circ}$ with the vertical are shown in the figure. Two block $A$ and $B$ are placed on the two planes. What is the relative vertical acceleration of $A$ with respect to $B$ ?
question: Two fixed frictionless inclined plane making an angle $30^{\circ}$ and $60^{\circ}$ with the vertical are shown in the figure. Two block $A$ and $B$ are placed on the two planes. What is the relative vertical acceleration of $A$ with respect to $B$ ?
solution:
$m g \sin \theta=m a$
$\therefore \quad a=g \sin \theta$
where $\mathrm{a}$ is along the inclined plane
$\therefore \quad$ vertical component of acceleration is $\mathrm{g} \sin ^2 \theta$
$\therefore \quad$ relative vertical acceleration of $A$ with respect to $B$ is

subject: Physics
q: A tube of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is filled completely with an incompressible liquid of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> and closed at both ends. The tube is then rotated in horizontal plane about one of its ends with a uniform angular velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><msqrt><mi>F</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> be the force exerted by the liquid at the other end then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be _____ .
question: A tube of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is filled completely with an incompressible liquid of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> and closed at both ends. The tube is then rotated in horizontal plane about one of its ends with a uniform angular velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><msqrt><mi>F</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> be the force exerted by the liquid at the other end then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be _____ .
solution:
Considering an element of tube of length dx having mass dm at a distance of x from right end as shown in figure below.

Now, force acting of that element is F=∫dmω2x

Integrating the above relation for whole length of tube, we have 

 F=∫0LmLdxω2x

=mLω2L22 =mω2L2

⇒ω=2mLF  =20.25×0.5F

=16F=4F rad s-1

Hence, the value of x=4.

subject: Physics
q: A block of weight $W$ rests on a horizontal floor with coefficient of static friction $\mu$. It is desired to make the block move by applying minimum amount of force. The angle $\theta$ from the horizontal at which the force should be applied and magnitude of the force $F$ are respectively.
question: A block of weight $W$ rests on a horizontal floor with coefficient of static friction $\mu$. It is desired to make the block move by applying minimum amount of force. The angle $\theta$ from the horizontal at which the force should be applied and magnitude of the force $F$ are respectively.
solution:
Let the force $F$ is applied at an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal.
For horizontal equilibrium, $F \cos \theta=\mu R$ For vertical equilibrium, $R+F \sin \theta=\mathrm{mg}$ or, $R=m g-F \sin \theta$ Substituting this value of $R$ in eq. (i), we get $$ \begin{aligned} & F \cos \theta=\mu(\mathrm{mg}-F \sin \theta) \\ & =\mu \mathrm{mg}-\mu F \sin \theta \\ & \text { or, } F(\cos \theta+\mu \sin \theta)=\mu \mathrm{mg} \\ & \text { or, } \mathrm{F}=\frac{\mu \mathrm{mg}}{\cos \theta+\mu \sin \theta} \end{aligned} $$ For $F$ to be minimum, the denominator $(\cos \theta+\mu \sin \theta)$ should be maximum. $$ \therefore \frac{d}{d \theta}(\cos \theta+\mu \sin \theta)=0 $$ or, $-\sin \theta+\mu \cos \theta=0$ or, $\tan \theta=\mu$ or, $\theta=\tan ^{-1}(\mu)$ Then, $\sin \theta=\frac{\mu}{\sqrt{1+\mu^2}}$ and $$ \cos \theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\mu^2}} $$ Hence, $F_{\text {min }}$ $$ =\frac{\mu w}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\mu^2}}+\frac{\mu^2}{\sqrt{1+\mu^2}}}=\frac{\mu w}{\sqrt{1+\mu^2}} $$

subject: Physics
q: A smooth block is released at rest on a $45^{\circ}$ incline and then slides a distance d. The time taken to slide is $\mathrm{n}$ times as much to slide on rough incline than on a smooth incline. The coefficient of friction is
question: A smooth block is released at rest on a $45^{\circ}$ incline and then slides a distance d. The time taken to slide is $\mathrm{n}$ times as much to slide on rough incline than on a smooth incline. The coefficient of friction is
solution:
$\mathrm{d}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{g}}{\sqrt{2}} \mathrm{t}_1^2$
$\mathrm{~d}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{g}}{\sqrt{2}}\left(1-\mu_{\mathrm{k}}\right) \mathrm{t}_2^2$
$\frac{\mathrm{t}_2^2}{\mathrm{t}_1^2}=\mathrm{n}^2=\frac{1}{1-\mu_{\mathrm{k}}}$

subject: Physics
q: One end of a massless spring of spring constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> and natural length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is fixed while the other end is connected to a small object of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> lying on a frictionless table. The spring remains horizontal on the table. If the object is made to rotate at an angular velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ω</mi></math> about an axis passing trough fixed end, then the elongation of the spring will be
question: One end of a massless spring of spring constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> and natural length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is fixed while the other end is connected to a small object of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> lying on a frictionless table. The spring remains horizontal on the table. If the object is made to rotate at an angular velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ω</mi></math> about an axis passing trough fixed end, then the elongation of the spring will be
solution:
The particle is moving in a horizontal circle, so it is accelerated towards the centre with magnitude v2r. The horizontal force on the particle is due to the spring and is given by kx, where x is the elongation and k is the spring constant.

kx=mv2r=mω2r=mω2l0+x

⇒k-mω2x=mω2l0

⇒x=mω2l0k-mω2

q: A balloon has mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> in air. The air escapes from the balloon at a uniform rate with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the balloon shrinks in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> completely. Then, the average force acting on that balloon will be (in dyne).
question: A balloon has mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> in air. The air escapes from the balloon at a uniform rate with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the balloon shrinks in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> completely. Then, the average force acting on that balloon will be (in dyne).
solution:
Given that the mass of the air m=10 g and it escapes in time t=5 s with velocity v=4.5 cm s-1.

The average force on the balloon can be given as,

F=vdmdt

⇒F=4.5×105=9 dyne

q:
This question has Statement 1 and Statement 2 . Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement 1: If you push on a cart being pulled by a horse so that it does not move, the cart pushes you back with an equal and opposite force.
Statement 2: The cart does not move because the force described in statement 1 cancel each other.
question:
This question has Statement 1 and Statement 2 . Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement 1: If you push on a cart being pulled by a horse so that it does not move, the cart pushes you back with an equal and opposite force.
Statement 2: The cart does not move because the force described in statement 1 cancel each other.
options: Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is the correct explanation of Statement 1.
Statement 1 is false, Statement 2 is true.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is false.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is not the correct explanation of Statement 1.
solution: According to newton third law of motion i.e. every action is associated with equal and opposite reaction.

q: As shown in the figure a block of mass 10 kg lying on a horizontal surface is pulled by a force F acting at an angle 30°, with horizontal. For μs=0.25, the block will just start to move for the value of F: [Given g = 10 m·s–2]
question: As shown in the figure a block of mass 10 kg lying on a horizontal surface is pulled by a force F acting at an angle 30°, with horizontal. For μs=0.25, the block will just start to move for the value of F: [Given g = 10 m·s–2]
solution:
The free body diagram for the given scenario is shown below-

From the equilibrium of the vertical component of forces, it can be written that

N=mg-F sin 30°=mg-F2=100-F2=200-F2........................(1)

From the equilibrium of horizontal component of forces, it can be written that,

F cos 30°= μN......................(2)

Substitute the expression for the normal reaction force from equation (1) into equation (2) and solve to calculate the value of the applied force.

3F2=0.25×200-F243F=200-FF=20043+1≈25.2

subject: Physics
q: A small block starts slipping down from a point B on an inclined plane AB, which is making an angle θ with the horizontal section BC is smooth and the remaining section CA is rough with a coefficient of friction μ. It is found that the block comes to rest as it reaches the bottom (point A) of the inclined plane. If BC=2AC, the coefficient of friction is given by μ=k tanθ. The value of k is .......
question: A small block starts slipping down from a point B on an inclined plane AB, which is making an angle θ with the horizontal section BC is smooth and the remaining section CA is rough with a coefficient of friction μ. It is found that the block comes to rest as it reaches the bottom (point A) of the inclined plane. If BC=2AC, the coefficient of friction is given by μ=k tanθ. The value of k is .......
solution:
Let AC=ℓ  ∴  BC=2ℓ  ∴  AB=3ℓ

Apply work – Energy theorem

Wt+Wmg=ΔKE

mg(3ℓ)sinθ-μmgcosθ(ℓ)=0+0

μmgcosθℓ=3mg/sinθ

μ=3tanθ=ktanθ

k=3

q: A block of mass ' $\mathrm{m}$ ' is connected to another block of mass ' $\mathrm{M}$ ' by a spring (massless) of spring constant ' $\mathrm{k}$ '. The blocks are kept on a smooth horizontal plane. Initially the blocks are at rest and the spring is unstretched. Then a constant force '$\mathrm{F}$' starts acting on the block of mass '$\mathrm{M}$' to pull it. Find the force on the block of mass '$\mathrm{m}$'
question: A block of mass ' $\mathrm{m}$ ' is connected to another block of mass ' $\mathrm{M}$ ' by a spring (massless) of spring constant ' $\mathrm{k}$ '. The blocks are kept on a smooth horizontal plane. Initially the blocks are at rest and the spring is unstretched. Then a constant force '$\mathrm{F}$' starts acting on the block of mass '$\mathrm{M}$' to pull it. Find the force on the block of mass '$\mathrm{m}$'
solution: $\mathrm{Kx}=\mathrm{ma}=\frac{\mathrm{mF}}{\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{M}}$

question: Three blocks A, B and C are pulled on a horizontal smooth surface by a force of 80 N as shown in figure. The tensions T1 and T2 in the string are respectively:
solution:
Since, the length of the string will remain same. Hence, all blocks will have the same acceleration.

aA=aB=aC=F5+3+2=8010=8 m s-2

Therefore, T1=5×8=40 N.

Now,

T2-T1=3×8⇒T2=64 N

solution:
Maximum kinetic friction acting, fk=μN=0.04×20g=8 Newton

Therefore, acceleration of the system will bea=60−826=2 m s-2

subject: Physics
q: A bullet of mass $4 \mathrm{~g}$ is fired horizontally with a speed of $300 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ into $0.8 \mathrm{~kg}$ block of wood at rest on a table. If the coefficient of friction between the block and the table is $0.3$, how far will the block slide approximately?
question: A bullet of mass $4 \mathrm{~g}$ is fired horizontally with a speed of $300 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ into $0.8 \mathrm{~kg}$ block of wood at rest on a table. If the coefficient of friction between the block and the table is $0.3$, how far will the block slide approximately?
solution:
Given, $\mathrm{m}_1=4 \mathrm{~g}, \mathrm{u}_1=300 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ $\mathrm{m}_2=0.8 \mathrm{~kg}=800 \mathrm{~g}, \mathrm{u}_2=0 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ From law of conservation of momentum, $\mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{u}_1+\mathrm{m}_2 \mathrm{u}_2=\mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{v}_1+\mathrm{m}_2 \mathrm{v}_2$ Let the velocity of combined system $=\mathrm{v} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ then, $4 \times 300+800 \times 0=(800+4) \times v$ $\mathrm{v}=\frac{1200}{804}=1.49 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
Now, $\mu=0.3$ (given)
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\mathrm{a}=\mu \mathrm{g} \\
&\mathrm{a}=0.3 \times 10\left(\text { take } \mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right) \\
&=3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$
then, from $\mathrm{v}^2=\mathrm{u}^2+2 \mathrm{as}$
$$
(1.49)^2=0+2 \times 3 \times s
$$

$\mathrm{s}=\frac{\left(1.49^2\right)}{6} ; \mathrm{s}=\frac{2.22}{6}=0.379 \mathrm{~m}$

q:
Statement I: A cyclist is moving on an unbanked road with a speed of 7 km h-1 and takes a sharp circular turn along a path of the radius of 2 m without reducing the speed. The static friction coefficient is 0.2. The cyclist will not slip and pass the curve g=9.8 m s-2

Statement II : If the road is banked at an angle of 45°, cyclist can cross the curve of 2 m radius with the speed of 18.5 km h-1 without slipping. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Statement I: A cyclist is moving on an unbanked road with a speed of 7 km h-1 and takes a sharp circular turn along a path of the radius of 2 m without reducing the speed. The static friction coefficient is 0.2. The cyclist will not slip and pass the curve g=9.8 m s-2

Statement II : If the road is banked at an angle of 45°, cyclist can cross the curve of 2 m radius with the speed of 18.5 km h-1 without slipping. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
options: Statement I is incorrect and statement II is correct

Statement I is correct and statement II is
incorrect
Both statement I and statement II are false
Both statement I and statement II are true
solution:
Statement Ivmax=μRg=0.2×2×9.8

vmax=1.97 m s-1

7 km h-1=1.944 m s-1

Speed is lower than vmax, hence it can take safe
turn.

Statement II 

vmax=Rgtanθ+μ1-μtanθ

=2×9.81+0.21-0.2=5.42 m s-1

18.5 km h-1=5.14 m s-1

Speed is lower than vmax, hence it can take safe turn.

q:
Two bodies of masses m1=5 kg and m2=3 kg are connected by a light string going over a smooth light pulley on a smooth inclined plane as shown in the figure. The system is at rest. The force exerted by the inclined plane on the body of mass m1 will be : [Take g=10 m s-2]

question:
Two bodies of masses m1=5 kg and m2=3 kg are connected by a light string going over a smooth light pulley on a smooth inclined plane as shown in the figure. The system is at rest. The force exerted by the inclined plane on the body of mass m1 will be : [Take g=10 m s-2]

​​​​​​​
solution:
The forces acting on two masses are shown below.

Here, N is normal to the incline and tension T along the string up the incline. As the string and the pulley are all light and smooth, the tension in the string is uniform everywhere.

​​​​​​​

For equilibrium condition, forces should add to zero.

Thus, equating, m2g=m1gsinθ

⇒sinθ=m2m1=35 and cosθ=45

Normal force on m1 is N=m1gcosθ=5gcosθ

=5×10×45=40 N

q:
A block of mass 200 g is kept stationary on a smooth inclined plane by applying a minimum horizontal force F=x N as shown in figure.

The value of x=_____.
question:
A block of mass 200 g is kept stationary on a smooth inclined plane by applying a minimum horizontal force F=x N as shown in figure.

The value of x=_____.
solution:
Balancing the forces in horizontal direction Nsinθ=F

And balancing the forces in vertical direction Ncosθ=mg

⇒tanθ=Fmg⇒F=mgtanθ

F=0.2×10×3=12 N. Therefore, x=12

60×7464000+20000=V

The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> will be _______.(Round off to the Nearest Integer) [Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> ]
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> rests on a horizontal floor with which it has a coefficient of static friction <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></mfrac></math>. It is desired to make the body move by applying the minimum possible force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> will be _______.(Round off to the Nearest Integer) [Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> ]
solution:
Fcosθ=μN

⇒Fsinθ+N=mg

⇒F=μmgcosθ+μsinθ

Fmin=μmg1+μ2=13×1023=5

subject: Physics
q: A ball is released from rest from point P of a smooth semi-spherical vessel as shown in figure. The ratio of the centripetal force and normal reaction on the ball at point Q is A while angular position of point Q is α with respect to point P. Which of the following graphs represent the correct relation between A and α when ball goes from Q to R?
question: A ball is released from rest from point P of a smooth semi-spherical vessel as shown in figure. The ratio of the centripetal force and normal reaction on the ball at point Q is A while angular position of point Q is α with respect to point P. Which of the following graphs represent the correct relation between A and α when ball goes from Q to R?
solution:
At angular position α as shown in the figure, applying conservation of mechanical energy

mgRsinα=12mv2⇒mv2R=2mgsinα

In the radial direction,

N-mgsinα=mv2R⇒N=3mgsinα

Now the ratio of centripetal force and normal reaction is,

A=mv2RN=2mgsinα3mgsinα=23.  Therefore, A is constant.

q: An insect crawls up a hemispherical surface very slowly. The coefficient of friction between the insect and the surface is $1 / 3$. If the line joining the centre of the hemispherical surface to the insect makes an angle $\alpha$ with the vertical, the maximum possible value of $\alpha$ so that the insect does not slip is given by
question: An insect crawls up a hemispherical surface very slowly. The coefficient of friction between the insect and the surface is $1 / 3$. If the line joining the centre of the hemispherical surface to the insect makes an angle $\alpha$ with the vertical, the maximum possible value of $\alpha$ so that the insect does not slip is given by
solution: The insect crawls up the bowl upto a certain height $h$ only till the component of its weight along the bowl is balanced by limiting frictional force. For limiting condition at point $\mathrm{A}$ $R=m g \cos \alpha$ $F_1=m g \sin \alpha$ Dividing eq. (ii) by (i) $\tan \alpha=\frac{1}{\cot \alpha}=\frac{F_1}{R}=\mu\left[\right.$ As $\left.F_1=\mu R\right]$ $\Rightarrow \quad \tan \alpha=\mu=\frac{1}{3}\left[\because \mu=\frac{1}{3}(\right.$ Given $\left.]\right)$ $\therefore \quad \cot \alpha=3$

solution: Chain block system is in equilibrium so $\mathrm{T}=200+100=300 \mathrm{~N} \text {. }$

q:
A boy of mass 4 kg is standing on a piece of wood having mass 5 kg. If the coefficient of friction between the wood and the floor is 0.5 , the maximum force that the boy can exert on the rope so that the piece of wood does not move from its place is _______ N. (Round off to the Nearest Integer)

[Take g=10 m s-2
question:
A boy of mass 4 kg is standing on a piece of wood having mass 5 kg. If the coefficient of friction between the wood and the floor is 0.5 , the maximum force that the boy can exert on the rope so that the piece of wood does not move from its place is _______ N. (Round off to the Nearest Integer)

[Take g=10 m s-2
solution:
N+T=90

T=μN=0.5(90-T)

1.5T=45

T=30 N

subject: Physics
q: A spring of force constant $15 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$ is cut into two pieces. If the ratio of their length is $1: 3$, then the force constant of smaller piece is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{N} / \mathrm{m}$.
question: A spring of force constant $15 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$ is cut into two pieces. If the ratio of their length is $1: 3$, then the force constant of smaller piece is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{N} / \mathrm{m}$.
solution:
Spring force constant is inversely proportional to length: $k \propto \frac{1}{L}$
So $kL = $ constant for a given spring material and structure.
Original spring: $k = 15$ N/m, length $= L$.
Smaller piece has length $\frac{L}{4}$ (since length ratio is 1:3, smaller is $\frac{1}{4}$ of total).
For smaller piece: $k_1 \times \frac{L}{4} = 15 \times L$
$k_1 = 15 \times 4 = 60$ N/m

subject: Physics
q: A block takes $t$ time to slide down a plane inclined at $45°$ to the horizontal. If the surface is made smooth (frictionless), the block takes time $\dfrac{t}{2}$ to slide down the plane. The coefficient of friction between the block and the inclined plane is $\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{100}\right)$. The value of $\alpha$ is __________.
question: A block takes $t$ time to slide down a plane inclined at $45°$ to the horizontal. If the surface is made smooth (frictionless), the block takes time $\dfrac{t}{2}$ to slide down the plane. The coefficient of friction between the block and the inclined plane is $\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{100}\right)$. The value of $\alpha$ is __________.
solution:
Let the length of the inclined plane be $s$.

For the smooth inclined plane, the acceleration of the block is $a_1 = g \sin \theta$.

The time taken is $t_1 = \dfrac{t}{2}$. Using the second equation of motion $s = ut + \dfrac{1}{2}at^2$ with $u = 0$:

$s = \dfrac{1}{2} (g \sin \theta) \left(\dfrac{t}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{8} g \sin \theta t^2$

For the rough inclined plane, the acceleration of the block is $a_2 = g(\sin \theta - \mu \cos \theta)$.

The time taken is $t_2 = t$. Using the equation of motion again:

$s = \dfrac{1}{2} g(\sin \theta - \mu \cos \theta) t^2$

Equating the two expressions for $s$:

$\dfrac{1}{8} g \sin \theta t^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} g(\sin \theta - \mu \cos \theta) t^2$

$\dfrac{1}{4} \sin \theta = \sin \theta - \mu \cos \theta$

Given $\theta = 45^{\circ}$, we have $\sin 45^{\circ} = \cos 45^{\circ} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$. Substituting these values:

$\dfrac{1}{4} = 1 - \mu$

$\mu = 1 - \dfrac{1}{4} = \dfrac{3}{4} = 0.75$

We are given $\mu = \dfrac{\alpha}{100}$.

$\dfrac{\alpha}{100} = 0.75 \Rightarrow \alpha = 75$

Answer: $75$

subject: Physics
q: One end of a massless rope, which passes over a massless and frictionless pulley $P$ is tied to a hook $C$ while the other end is free. Maximum tension that the rope can bear is $360 \mathrm{~N}$. With what value of maximum safe acceleration (in $\mathrm{ms}^{-2}$ ) can a man of $60 \mathrm{~kg}$ climb on the rope?
question: One end of a massless rope, which passes over a massless and frictionless pulley $P$ is tied to a hook $C$ while the other end is free. Maximum tension that the rope can bear is $360 \mathrm{~N}$. With what value of maximum safe acceleration (in $\mathrm{ms}^{-2}$ ) can a man of $60 \mathrm{~kg}$ climb on the rope?
solution: $\mathrm{T}-60 \mathrm{~g}=60 \mathrm{a} ; \mathrm{T}=3000 \mathrm{~N} ; \quad \therefore \mathrm{a}=4 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$

question: A wooden block, initially at rest on the ground, is pushed by a force which increases linearly with time $t$. Which of the following curve best describes acceleration of the block with time:
solution: $\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{ma} \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\mathrm{kt}}{\mathrm{m}}$ a vs $t$ will be straight line passing through origin.
